Susana MacLean: All-star contributor Folks, the apartments being built are the result of a court order many years ago requiring Westfield to build hundreds of units of affordable housing which, in order to be profitable for developers, also necessitates additional market-priced apartments. Many other towns received the same court order. Westfield fought it and lost during the last Republican town administration. The Democratic mayor has had to oversee the town’s compliance with the court order. The reason for the court order in the first place is that the Supreme Court of NJ recognized that there is a dire shortage of affordable housing in the state and wealthy towns were refusing to allow such housing to be built within their borders. The comments in this thread are proof of residents’ resistance to such development. All of us who have children have contributed to an increase in the population. Where do we expect our kids to live as adults if there is insufficient housing? I’m nostalgic for Colonial Westfield, too, but that’s been slowly destroyed by McMansions springing up all over town, yet no-one complains about that. I think the apartment buildings are well-designed and I hope that they attract people who can shop and dine downtown and become part of our community.

Zoë Langsten McKelvey: All-star contributor It sounds like cars will still be able to enter the municipal lot from Elm Street, but not exit through Elm. That’s unfortunate, because I’m not sure it improves pedestrian safety. It *will* create terrible traffic on Mountain Avenue, already bottlenecked at rush hour, while endangering pedestrians crossing the parking lot entrance/exit on Mountain Ave. the parking lot will feel as congested as Trader Joe’s.

postText:
Eric Rodrigues
Also, if you park in your driveway you’ll generally be able to drive through the street day of. Milling / paving companies are used to coordinating those logistics for homeowners.
Once off your street they’re not really going to want you driving back until they wrap-up.
If you leave your car on the road day of, they’ll typically scan plates to see if you’re local so they can knock on your door to remove your car.
Otherwise they’ll tow it to a nearby street and - depending on if they have reasonable notice - issue a parking fine (but even that is not hugely common).
Best bet is to call Westfield DPW to ask what to expect since it’s hard to get reasonable advanced notice for paving during the summer months.

Adam Fine: Assuming your children have no chronic issues : The overall office ends up mattering more then the individual doctors(who are almost all great everywhere). I would pick based on convivence. Who has the quickest availability for sick visits? (usually a larger group instead of a small practice). Who has an around -the clock number for questions. Who has the easiest parking and is closest to where you live. For me that was Westfield Pediatrics - but they are a mixed bag. I always get sick visit appointments right away and they have their own parking lot. Some of their office and billing policies can get annoying (but the policies are clearly documented). The doctors are great but they don't do on site bloodwork ( do you really want to take a toddler to Quest or LabCorp?)
